text: <par>The original /təhúcháʔ/ is corrected and the STEDT's analysis is deleted.</par>
<par>The original /təhúhɨŋ/ is wrong. The English gloss `fingernail' is not appropriate.</par>

<par>The final glottal stop in Sak <latinform>ʃiʔ</latinform> has led Huziwara to speculate that the Proto-Asakian etymon had a final consonant, perhaps <latinform>*-r</latinform> (cf. <latinform>sor</latinform> < <xref ref="324">#324</xref>).</par>
<par>This word is a borrowing from Bengali.</par>
<par>This word is not Indo-Aryan origin. See the note above.</par>

This word looks like a borrowing from Bengali moric `pepper' at first glance, however, it shares the same etymon with other Luish/Asakian languages. Proto-Luish *m-ruk (?) is proposed in Huziwara (2012) and is also listed in Matisoff (2013).

<par>Sak initial <latinform>ky-</latinform> often corresponds to Kadu <latinform>ty-</latinform> or <latinform>ti-</latinform>, cf. Sak <latinform>a kyí</latinform>, Sak (Bawtala) <latinform>ă tji²</latinform>, Sak (Dodem) <latinform>ă tji⁴</latinform>, and Kadu <latinform>ti¹</latinform> 'penis'.</par>
<par>The original /apá/ is `elder brother'. `brothers' is added to the gloss.</par>
<par>The unstressed first syllable of this form might be a reduction of <xref ref="2753">#2753</xref>.</par>
<par>This form is probably a borrowing from Burmese.</par>
<par>The original /mwáiŋ/ is a borrowing from Marma and rarely used.</par>
<par>Possible loan from Indo-Aryan; cf. forms like Hindi mirich.</par>
<par>This word is not Indo-Aryan. See my note above.</par>
<par>The original /ŋərouʔmǔ/ should be /ŋərouʔmǔŋ/ and it means `black-pepper powder'.</par>
<par>Possible loan from Indo-Aryan; cf. forms like Hindi mirich.</par>
This word is a Marma form for WrB nga-rut `pepper / capsicum' and probably not Indo-Aryan.
